Project Python Foundations: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Marks: 60

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

In [248]:
# Installing the libraries with the specified version.
!pip install numpy==2.0.2 pandas==2.2.2 matplotlib==3.10.0 seaborn==0.13.2 -q --user
In [249]:
# import libraries for data manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Understanding the structure of the data¶

In [250]:
# uncomment and run the following lines for Google Colab
# from google.colab import drive
# drive.mount('/content/drive')
In [251]:
# read the data
df = pd.read_csv('foodhub_order.csv')
# returns the first 5 rows
df.head()
Out[251]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24

Observations:¶

The DataFrame has 9 columns as mentioned in the Data Dictionary. Data in each row corresponds to the order placed by a customer.

Question 2: What are the datatypes of the different columns in the dataset? (The info() function can be used) [0.5 mark]¶

In [253]:
# Use info() to print a concise summary of the DataFrame
df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 1898 entries, 0 to 1897
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column                 Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------                 --------------  -----  
 0   order_id               1898 non-null   int64  
 1   customer_id            1898 non-null   int64  
 2   restaurant_name        1898 non-null   object 
 3   cuisine_type           1898 non-null   object 
 4   cost_of_the_order      1898 non-null   float64
 5   day_of_the_week        1898 non-null   object 
 6   rating                 1898 non-null   object 
 7   food_preparation_time  1898 non-null   int64  
 8   delivery_time          1898 non-null   int64  
dtypes: float64(1), int64(4), object(4)
memory usage: 133.6+ KB

Observations:¶

  • All the columns have 1898 observations indicating there are no missing values.
  • 'rating' column is being read as a 'object' data type but it should be in 'float' or 'int' format.
  • 'day_of_the_week' column is object type can changed to categorical type as categorical columns help to improve the processing speed and reduces the storage space when we are working with a large dataset and also as day_of_the_week week has only 2 repeating values 'weekday' or 'weekend'.

Question 4: Check the statistical summary of the data. What is the minimum, average, and maximum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed? [2 marks]¶

In [256]:
# Write your code here
df.describe()
Out[256]:
order_id customer_id cost_of_the_order food_preparation_time delivery_time
count 1.898000e+03 1898.000000 1898.000000 1898.000000 1898.000000
mean 1.477496e+06 171168.478398 16.498851 27.371970 24.161749
std 5.480497e+02 113698.139743 7.483812 4.632481 4.972637
min 1.476547e+06 1311.000000 4.470000 20.000000 15.000000
25% 1.477021e+06 77787.750000 12.080000 23.000000 20.000000
50% 1.477496e+06 128600.000000 14.140000 27.000000 25.000000
75% 1.477970e+06 270525.000000 22.297500 31.000000 28.000000
max 1.478444e+06 405334.000000 35.410000 35.000000 33.000000
In [257]:
#df.describe().T
In [258]:
df.head()
Out[258]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24

Observations:¶

the minimum time it takes for the food to be prepared once it is ordered is around 20 minutes which kind of suggests that even the fastest restaurants or quick orders have a certain base time period needed for cooking and packing the food. the maximum time it takes for the food to be prepared once it is ordered is around 35 minutes which is not too far from the mean, showing no concerns of extreme delays or long preparation times in the dataset. the average time it takes for the food to be prepared once it is ordered is around 27 minutes indicating that most orders take a moderate and predictable time to prepare food once the order is placed.

Data columns (total 9 columns):

memory usage: 133.6+ KB

Observations:¶

there 736 orders which are not ratedby customers which is a significant portion of the dataset of 1898 entries. also indicating that many customers choose not to provide ratings for their orders. The high number of missing ratings suggests that ratings alone may not fully represent customer satisfaction levels. we need to explore whether these unrated orders are linked to longer delivery times, higher preparation times, or specific restaurants, as this may reveal hidden patterns in customer behavior.

observation for food_preparation_time:¶

In [277]:
# food_preparation_time

sns.histplot(data =df , x= 'food_preparation_time')
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data= df , x= 'food_preparation_time')
plt.show()

Observations for Food preparation time :

  • The plots show a uniform and symmetric distribution, with preparation time evenly spread between 20 and 35 minutes.
  • The boxplot confirms the consistency in the food prep time also indicating there no major outliers and we see a median positioned near the center of the range, indicating stable and predictable preparation times for orders.

Observations for Cost_of_the_order¶

In [278]:
#cost_of_the_order

sns.histplot(data =df , x= 'cost_of_the_order')
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data= df , x= 'cost_of_the_order')
plt.show()

Observations for Cost_of_the_order :

  • The distribution is right-skewed with most orders falling in the low to mid price range and few orders of higher costs.
  • Both the plots here show that while the average cost of orders are moderate but there are also a small number of customers who have placed significantly expensive orders

Observations for delivery_time¶

In [279]:
#delivery_time

sns.histplot(data =df , x= 'delivery_time')
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data= df , x= 'delivery_time')
plt.show()

Observations for Delivery time:

  • we can see that distribution is fairly broad but slightly right-skewed, with most deliveries occurring between 20 and 30 minutes.
  • The boxplot shows that while most delivery times are around in mid 20 minute delivery time, there are some orders with higher delivery times that stand out, suggesting occasional delays.

Observations for Rating¶

In [280]:
#rating
sns.countplot(data=df, x='rating', hue='rating')

plt.show()
In [281]:
df['rating'].value_counts(normalize= True)
Out[281]:
proportion
rating
Not given 0.387777
5 0.309800
4 0.203372
3 0.099052

Observations for Rating :

  • Major distribution for rating is concentrated at the higher end, with most customers with 4 or 5 star rating indicating positive customer satisfaction but we see a few lower ratings around 3 indicate some dissatisfaction on certain orders by customers.
  • It will be interesting to examine whether longer delivery times or higher order costs are linked to these lower customer ratings.

About 29-30 percent of orders in the dataset cost more than 20 dollars.

correlation matrix for numeric columns¶

In [309]:
# Select numeric columns
numeric_cols =['cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time', 'delivery_time']
corr = df[numeric_cols].corr()
sns.heatmap(corr, annot= True, vmax=1, vmin= -1)
plt.show()
In [310]:
sns.pairplot(df[numeric_cols])
plt.show()

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [311]:
# Write the code here
df_rated=df[df['rating'] != 'Not given'].copy()
df_rated['rating'] =df_rated['rating'].astype(float)
df_rated.info()

Observations:¶

Based on the criteria only 4 restaurants qualify to get the promotional offer

  • These restaurants not only have a large number of customer ratings but also maintain an excellent average rating above 4, indicating high customer satisfaction and good service quality.

  • they are popular and consistent. Featuring them in advertisements is likely to attract more customers and enhance the brand's reputation

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [317]:
# Write the code
def revenue(x):
  if x > 20:
    return 0.25*x
  elif x > 5:
    return 0.15*x
  else:
    return 0
In [318]:
df['revenue']= df['cost_of_the_order'].apply(revenue)
df['revenue']
Out[318]:
revenue
0 7.6875
1 1.8120
2 1.8345
3 7.3000
4 1.7385
... ...
1893 5.5775
1894 1.8270
1895 6.3050
1896 1.8270
1897 2.9175

1898 rows × 1 columns

np.float64(6166.303)

Observations:¶

The company generates a net revenue of approximately 6,166 dollars on all orders. a major portion of orders fall into the higher value brackets,those above $20 are contributing more due to the larger commission percentage.

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [321]:
# Write the code
df['total_time']=df['food_preparation_time']+df['delivery_time']
df.head()
Out[321]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time revenue total_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20 7.6875 45
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23 1.8120 48
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28 1.8345 51
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15 7.3000 40
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24 1.7385 49
In [322]:
df_more_than_60=df[df['total_time']>60]
num_orders_morethan_60=df_more_than_60.shape[0]
In [323]:
num_orders_morethan_60/df.shape[0]*100
Out[323]:
10.537407797681771

Observations:¶

This indicates that the majority of orders are delivered within 60 minutes, a notable portion over one tenth of orders have delays beyond the 60-minute mark.

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [324]:
# Write the code here
df.groupby(['day_of_the_week'])['delivery_time'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
Out[324]:
delivery_time
day_of_the_week
Weekday 28.340037
Weekend 22.470022

Observations:

Interestingly delivery is faster on weekends compared to weekdays the difference in averege is quite noticable. there may be several reasons affecting during the weekdays like traffic or fewer delivery partners.

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

  1. Customer Satisfaction Most of the ratings fall on the higher side,showing positive customer feedback.

there were only four restaurants that met criteria of number of rating more than 50 and the average rating greater than 4 indicating that consistent high quality service is difficult to maintain.

  1. Popular Cuisines

Some cuisines are quite popular over the weekends, showing the rise in demand for certain food types based on the day of the week.

Weekend preferences differ from weekday patterns, offering opportunities for targeted promotions.

  1. Delivery Performance

Only 10.54% of orders take more than 60 minutes, showing operational efficiency overall.

weekday delivery times are slower (average 28 min) compared to weekends (average 22 min), likely due to traffic and other challenges

  1. Revenue Insights

High-value orders which are above $20 contribute a larger share of revenue due to the 25% commission bracket.

  1. Customer Ordering Patterns

only a small group of customers have turned up placing orders again, showing a potential for loyalty programs

Recommendations:¶

  1. Partnering with Top Performing Restaurants by using them as benchmark examples for other partners to improve the efficiency.

  2. To improve delivery efficiency by

  • Encouraging restaurants to pre-prep high-demand items during peak hours

  • Optimizing routing during congested time windows.

  • increasing delivery partner availability during peak office hours

  • Add "order ready" time-tracking to minimize customer wait time

  1. Running cuisine based promotions

  • Run targeted weekend promotions on the most popular weekend cuisines.

  • Offer bundle or combo deals for high-demand cuisine types

  1. Offer loyalty points, cashback, or exclusive discounts to high-frequency customers to retain loyal customer base.

  2. Improve Restaurant Rating Practices by

  • Encouraging customers to rate their orders by offering small incentives.

  • Provide restaurants with rating insights to help them improve weak areas.

  • Identify restaurants with many low ratings and conduct quality audits.
